subject: ap-chemistry
title: Methanol Decomposition: Thermodynamics & Equilibrium
stimulus:
$\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}(g) + 2\text{H}_2(g)$, $\Delta H^\circ_{rxn} = +90.0$ kJ/mol

Methanol vapor decomposes to form carbon monoxide gas and hydrogen gas at high temperatures in the presence of a platinum catalyst, as represented by the balanced chemical equation given.

parts.prompt: Are the hydrogen atoms oxidized or are they reduced in the forward reaction? Justify your answer in terms of oxidation numbers.
parts.scoring_notes: In $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$: each H bonded to C has oxidation state +1 (or −1 if using C–H convention, but College Board uses H = +1 in organic by default unless bonded to metal); the H bonded to O has oxidation state +1. In $\text{H}_2$: each H has oxidation state 0. Since H goes from +1 to 0, hydrogen atoms gain electrons and are reduced. (Accept justification using either C–H bond H = +1 or H = −1 if internally consistent, but the standard AP answer is H is reduced: +1 → 0.)

scoring_points.description: For 'reduced' with correct oxidation number change (+1 → 0): 1 point

required_elements: Oxidation number of H changes from +1 (in $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) to 0 (in $\text{H}_2$).
alternatives.correct_example: The hydrogen atoms are reduced. In $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, hydrogen has an oxidation number of +1 (bonded to the electronegative oxygen). In $\text{H}_2$, hydrogen has an oxidation number of 0. Because the oxidation number decreases from +1 to 0, hydrogen atoms gain electrons and are therefore reduced.

wrong_examples: Saying oxidized — a decrease in oxidation number (gaining electrons) is reduction, not oxidation.
Citing the carbon atom's change instead of hydrogen's — the question specifically asks about hydrogen.
common_traps: Oxidation = increase in oxidation number (lose electrons). Reduction = decrease in oxidation number (gain electrons). In $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, H is +1; in $\text{H}_2$, H is 0. Decrease from +1 to 0 = reduction.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer: 1 point. The H atoms are reduced because they change from an oxidation number of +1 to 0.

sample_responses.response: Reduced. In $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, H has an oxidation number of +1; in $\text{H}_2$, H has an oxidation number of 0. The decrease from +1 to 0 means H gains electrons — reduction.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct identification and correct oxidation-number change cited; matches SG.

sample_responses.response: Oxidized. H goes from +1 to 0, losing electrons.
sample_responses.commentary: A decrease in oxidation number is reduction (gaining electrons), not oxidation.

parts.prompt: In the following box, draw the complete Lewis electron-dot diagram for the carbon monoxide molecule in which every atom obeys the octet rule. Show all bonding and nonbonding valence electrons.
parts.scoring_notes: CO has a triple bond between C and O: $:C\equiv O:$. Carbon has 1 lone pair; oxygen has 1 lone pair. Total valence electrons = 4 + 6 = 10. Structure: lone pair on C, triple bond, lone pair on O. With a triple bond, both C and O achieve octets (C: 2 + 6 = 8; O: 2 + 6 = 8). Formal charge: C = 4 − 2 − 3 = −1; O = 6 − 2 − 3 = +1 (nonzero formal charges must be shown).

parts.prompt:
The values of the standard molar entropies of the compounds involved in the reaction are given in the following table.

| Substance | $S^\circ$ (J/(K·mol)) |
|---|---|
| $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g)$ | 240 |
| $\text{CO}(g)$ | 198 |
| $\text{H}_2(g)$ | 131 |

(i) Use the data in the table to calculate the value of the standard entropy change, $\Delta S^\circ_{rxn}$, in J/(K·mol), for the reaction.

(ii) Calculate the value of $\Delta G^\circ$, in kJ/mol, for the reaction at 375 K. Assume that $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ are independent of temperature.
parts.scoring_notes:
(i) $\Delta S^\circ_{rxn} = [S^\circ(\text{CO}) + 2S^\circ(\text{H}_2)] - S^\circ(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}) = [198 + 2(131)] - 240 = 460 - 240 = +220$ J/(K·mol). 
(ii) $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ = 90.0\text{ kJ/mol} - (375\text{ K})(0.220\text{ kJ/(K·mol)}) = 90.0 - 82.5 = +7.5$ kJ/mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct stoichiometry (may be implicit) AND correct calculated value: 2 points (award 1 if stoichiometry correct but arithmetic error, or stoichiometry wrong but method shown)
wrong_examples: Forgetting the coefficient 2 on $\text{H}_2$ — the balanced equation shows 2 mol $\text{H}_2$ produced.
Subtracting products from reactants instead of reactants from products — $\Delta S^\circ_{rxn} = \Sigma S^\circ_{prod} - \Sigma S^\circ_{react}$.
common_traps: $\Delta S^\circ_{rxn} = \Sigma n_p S^\circ_{products} - \Sigma n_r S^\circ_{reactants}$. Use stoichiometric coefficients. Here products are 1 mol CO + 2 mol $\text{H}_2$; reactant is 1 mol $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct stoichiometry (may be implicit): 1 point. For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $\Delta S^\circ = 198 + 2(131) - 240 = +220$ J/(K·mol).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct stoichiometry and calculation; matches SG answer.
Forgot to multiply $S^\circ(\text{H}_2)$ by its stoichiometric coefficient of 2.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated $\Delta G^\circ$ at 375 K: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$
$= 90.0\text{ kJ/mol} - (375\text{ K})(0.220\text{ kJ/(K·mol)})$
$= 90.0 - 82.5 = +7.5$ kJ/mol

(Must convert $\Delta S^\circ$ from J to kJ: 220 J/K·mol = 0.220 kJ/K·mol)

wrong_examples: Using $\Delta S^\circ = 220$ without converting to kJ — units must be consistent (both kJ or both J).
Using $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ + T\Delta S^\circ$ — the Gibbs equation has a minus sign: $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$.
common_traps: $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$. T must be in Kelvin. Convert $\Delta S^\circ$ to kJ/(K·mol) before substituting. A positive $\Delta G^\circ$ at 375 K means the reaction is not spontaneous at this temperature.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $\Delta G^\circ = 90.0\text{ kJ} - (375\text{ K})(0.220\text{ kJ/(K·mol)}) = +7.5$ kJ/mol.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct Gibbs calculation with proper unit conversion; matches SG.
Failed to convert $\Delta S^\circ$ from J to kJ; 220 J/(K·mol) = 0.220 kJ/(K·mol).

parts.prompt: Use information from the particle diagram to calculate the partial pressure of CO at equilibrium when the total pressure of the equilibrium mixture is 12.0 atm.
parts.scoring_notes: From the particle diagram, the mole fraction of CO must be determined by counting particles. Based on the 2022 exam diagram, there are 3 CO particles and 6 $\text{H}_2$ particles out of a total of 10 particles (including 1 $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$). Mole fraction of CO = 3/10. $P_{\text{CO}} = \chi_{\text{CO}} \times P_{total} = (3/10)(12.0) = 3.6$ atm. (Consistent with exam answer key.)

scoring_points.description: For the correct partial pressure of CO consistent with the particle diagram: 1 point

required_elements: $P_{\text{CO}} = \chi_{\text{CO}} \times P_{total}$; correct mole fraction from particle diagram × 12.0 atm
alternatives.correct_example:
From the particle diagram: count the CO particles and total particles. Mole fraction of CO $= n_{\text{CO}} / n_{total}$.
$P_{\text{CO}} = \chi_{\text{CO}} \times 12.0\text{ atm} = 3.6\text{ atm}$

(Accept answer consistent with correct reading of the diagram.)

wrong_examples: Using total pressure as the CO partial pressure — must multiply by mole fraction.
Counting all particles as the same type — distinguish $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$, CO, and $\text{H}_2$ from the diagram.
common_traps: Dalton's Law: $P_i = \chi_i \times P_{total}$. Read the particle count for each species from the diagram; compute mole fraction of CO; multiply by 12.0 atm.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $P_{\text{CO}} = \chi_{\text{CO}} \times P_{total} = (3/10)(12.0) = 3.6$ atm (accept answer consistent with correct reading of particle diagram).

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly counts CO particles from diagram and applies Dalton's Law.
Must use mole fraction of CO; total pressure applies to all gas species combined.

parts.prompt:
Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, $K_p$, for the reaction.

$\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}(g) + 2\text{H}_2(g)$
parts.scoring_notes: $K_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2}^2}{P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}$. Partial pressures raised to stoichiometric coefficients; products over reactants.

scoring_points.description: For the correct $K_p$ expression with correct exponents and products over reactants: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$K_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2}^2}{P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}}$

Products (CO and $\text{H}_2$) in numerator; $\text{H}_2$ raised to the power of 2 (stoichiometric coefficient). Reactant ($\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) in denominator.

wrong_examples: Writing $K_p = P_{\text{CO}} \cdot P_{\text{H}_2} / P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}$ without the exponent 2 on $P_{\text{H}_2}$ — the coefficient 2 must appear as an exponent.
Inverting (reactants over products) — $K_p$ places products in numerator.
common_traps: $K_p = \prod P_{products}^{coeff} / \prod P_{reactants}^{coeff}$. Each partial pressure is raised to its stoichiometric coefficient. $K_p$ uses pressures; $K_c$ uses concentrations.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct products-over-reactants expression with exponent 2 on $P_{\text{H}_2}$.
Missing exponent 2 on $P_{\text{H}_2}$; stoichiometric coefficient must be used as exponent.

parts.prompt:
Use the information in the table to calculate the value of the equilibrium constant, $K_p$, at the new temperature.

| Substance | Partial Pressure at Different Temperature |
|---|---|
| $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g)$ | 2.7 atm |
| $\text{CO}(g)$ | 4.2 atm |
| $\text{H}_2(g)$ | 8.4 atm |

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value of $K_p$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Inverting numerator and denominator — products go in numerator.
common_traps: Substitute directly from the table into the $K_p$ expression from part (e). Square $P_{\text{H}_2} = 8.4$ atm (gives 70.56) before multiplying by $P_{\text{CO}}$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $K_p = (4.2)(8.4)^2/(2.7) = 110$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct substitution with $P_{\text{H}_2}$ squared; matches SG.
$P_{\text{H}_2}$ must be squared (coefficient = 2); omitting the square changes the answer by a factor of 8.4.

parts.prompt: The volume of the container is rapidly doubled with no change in temperature. As equilibrium is re-established, does the number of moles of $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g)$ increase, decrease, or remain the same? Justify your answer by comparing the value of the reaction quotient, $Q$, with the value of the equilibrium constant, $K_p$.
parts.scoring_notes: When volume doubles, all partial pressures are halved. New pressures: $P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = 2.7/2 = 1.35$, $P_{\text{CO}} = 4.2/2 = 2.1$, $P_{\text{H}_2} = 8.4/2 = 4.2$ atm. $Q = \frac{(2.1)(4.2)^2}{1.35} = \frac{(2.1)(17.64)}{1.35} = \frac{37.0}{1.35} \approx 27.4$. Since $Q \approx 27.4 < K_p \approx 110$, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction (toward products), consuming $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ → moles of $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ decrease.

scoring_points.description: For a correct calculation showing $Q < K_p$ after volume doubling: 1 point

required_elements: After doubling volume, all partial pressures are halved.
alternatives.correct_example:
Doubling the volume halves all partial pressures:
$P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}} = 1.35$ atm, $P_{\text{CO}} = 2.1$ atm, $P_{\text{H}_2} = 4.2$ atm
$Q = \dfrac{(2.1)(4.2)^2}{1.35} = \dfrac{(2.1)(17.64)}{1.35} \approx 27 < K_p \approx 110$

wrong_examples: Not halving pressures after volume doubling — Boyle's Law: at constant T, doubling V halves P for each gas.
Calculating $Q$ with the original pressures — $Q$ must be calculated at the new conditions.
common_traps: When volume doubles at constant T and constant n: $P_{new} = P_{old}/2$ for each species (Boyle's Law). Calculate $Q$ with new pressures and compare to $K_p$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct comparison of Q and $K_p$: 1 point. After doubling volume, partial pressures halve: $Q = (P_{\text{CO}}/2)(P_{\text{H}_2}/2)^2/(P_{\text{CH}_3\text{OH}}/2) = K_p/4 \approx 27 < K_p$.

sample_responses.response: After volume doubles, all partial pressures halve. $Q = (2.1)(4.2)^2/(1.35) \approx 27 < K_p \approx 110$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly halves all pressures and calculates Q; comparison to Kp is valid.

sample_responses.response: Q = Kp because equilibrium hasn't shifted yet.
sample_responses.commentary: After volume change, concentrations/pressures change, so Q changes before equilibrium is re-established.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer ('decrease') and valid justification using Q vs. $K_p$: 1 point

required_elements: Because $Q < K_p$, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction, consuming $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$.
alternatives.correct_example: Since $Q < K_p$, the reaction proceeds forward to produce more CO and $\text{H}_2$, consuming $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$. Therefore, the number of moles of $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}(g)$ decreases as equilibrium is re-established.

wrong_examples: Saying $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ increases — $Q < K_p$ drives the forward reaction, which consumes $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$.
Citing Le Chatelier's principle without calculating $Q$ — the question explicitly asks for a $Q$ vs. $K_p$ comparison.
common_traps: $Q < K_p$: reaction proceeds forward (toward products). Forward direction consumes $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ (reactant) and produces CO and $\text{H}_2$. This is also consistent with Le Chatelier: increasing volume (decreasing pressure) favors the side with more moles of gas (products: 3 mol vs. reactant: 1 mol).
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point. Decrease. Given that $Q < K_p$, the partial pressures (moles) of products will increase as equilibrium re-establishes, decreasing the number of moles of $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$.

sample_responses.response: Decrease. Since $Q < K_p$, the forward reaction is favored, consuming $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ and producing more CO and $\text{H}_2$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct direction and correct Q vs. Kp justification; matches SG.

sample_responses.response: Increase. The volume increase gives $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$ more space.
sample_responses.commentary: Q < Kp drives the forward reaction, consuming CH₃OH; volume increase alone does not increase moles of CH₃OH.
